headline: OCSP-backed revocation checking joins certification-path validation.
summary: pki.path.ocspChecker is the OCSP counterpart to pki.path.crlChecker: it plugs into the same revocationChecker hook on pki.path.validate, so an operator can back path revocation with pre-fetched OCSP responses (a fetch result, or a response stapled into a TLS handshake) instead of, or alongside, CRLs. It verifies the RFC 6960 chain of trust end to end -- the response must bind to the certificate under test through the full CertID triple (recomputing issuerNameHash and issuerKeyHash under whichever hash algorithm the responder chose, so a good status for one issuer's serial cannot answer for another issuer's same serial), it must be signed by an authorized responder (the issuing CA directly, or a certificate that same CA issued bearing id-kp-OCSPSigning -- an ordinary leaf, an anyExtendedKeyUsage certificate, an expired responder, or one from a different CA cannot sign a status), the signature must verify over the exact response bytes, and it must be current against thisUpdate and nextUpdate. Every path that is not a fully authorized, current, cert-matching good collapses to an undetermined status that fails the validation closed unless the caller opts into soft-fail; a revoked status surfaces its revocation reason. It is transport-free -- it verifies bytes the caller supplies -- so nonce anti-replay stays a live-client concern and the residual replay defense is the thisUpdate/nextUpdate currency window.

Added: pki.path.ocspChecker(responses) -> RevocationChecker builds an OCSP-backed revocation checker for pki.path.validate's revocationChecker option from a set of pre-fetched OCSP responses (DER Buffer, PEM string, or already-parsed). It matches the full CertID triple against the certificate under whichever hash algorithm the CertID declares, authorizes the responder (issuing CA or a CA-issued id-kp-OCSPSigning delegate), verifies the response signature over tbsResponseData, and enforces thisUpdate/nextUpdate currency, reporting good, revoked (with revocationReason), or unknown. A wrong-issuer CertID, an unauthorized responder, a stale, not-yet-valid, or nextUpdate-less response, a non-successful responseStatus, or any verification failure yields unknown, which the validator fails closed unless softFail is set. RFC 6960.
